ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for char siew (Chinese BBQ pork); mapped to broader pork food product class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for char siew; A0CKW (pork meat, cooked) is the closest defensible parent match. Char siew is a marinated and roasted preparation not separately enumerated in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for char siew as a specific food concept.

serving.description: 1 small plate of sliced char siew
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of char siew is approximately 150 g, equivalent to a small plate of sliced barbecued pork as commonly served in Singapore hawker centres and Cantonese restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Singapore Food Composition Database — Char Siew (Barbecued Pork)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Char siew is listed as a named food item in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database. Per-100g values are sourced from HPB Foodo. Values for cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat were cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy, pork, cured, roasted) where HPB data were not available.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for char siew in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 50 (low) based on the analogous food 'pork, roasted with sweet marinade' and the known GI contribution of sugar-based glazes on low-GI protein foods. Carbohydrate content per 100g is 10g; GL per 150g serving = (50 × 15g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 7.5. Estimation basis documented per Veritas GI policy.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (533 mg per 100g) from soy sauce, oyster sauce, and hoisin sauce used in the marinade
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit char siew to small portions (≤75g) and avoid consuming the sauce or glaze, as a 150g serving provides approximately 800 mg sodium, representing over one-third of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load (10g per 100g) with high sugar content (8g per 100g) from honey and sugar-based glaze, contributing to postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume char siew in controlled portions (≤100g) and pair it with low-GI vegetables or wholegrains to moderate the glycemic response from the sugar-rich marinade.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (8g per 100g) and sodium (533 mg per 100g) alongside moderate cholesterol (60 mg per 100g) from fatty pork cuts
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit char siew to occasional consumption (≤100g per occasion), choose leaner pork cuts where available, and be mindful of cumulative saturated fat and sodium intake across the meal.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Char siew (叉烧, Chāshāo) is a classic Cantonese-style Chinese barbecued pork dish that is deeply embedded in the food culture of Guangdong, Hong Kong, and Singapore. The dish is made from pork shoulder, belly, or neck, marinated in a sweet and savoury mixture of soy sauce, hoisin sauce, oyster sauce, honey, sugar, five-spice powder, and optionally red fermented tofu, which imparts its characteristic reddish-brown colour. The marinated pork is traditionally roasted on hooks in a char siu oven at high heat, producing a caramelised, lacquered exterior with a juicy, tender interior. In Singapore, char siew is a staple hawker dish served sliced over rice (char siew rice) or noodles, or used as a filling in steamed buns (char siew bao). Nutritionally, char siew is a moderate-energy food providing approximately 300 kcal per 100g, with a high protein content (20g) and significant fat (20g, of which 8g is saturated). The sugar-rich glaze contributes 8g of sugar per 100g, and the sodium content is high at 533 mg per 100g due to the multiple salty sauces in the marinade. Char siew is not suitable for strict low-sodium or low-sugar diets. It is a good source of B vitamins including niacin, B6, and B12, which support energy metabolism and neurological function. The dish is not halal due to the use of pork and is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans.

What is char siew made of?

confidence_reason: Char siew is a named food item in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (primary approved source). Core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are sourced from HPB Foodo with cross-reference to USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat. GI is estimated from an analogous food in the University of Sydney GI Database with documented basis.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for char siew is not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy pork roasted entry as of 2026-04-16. water_g set to -1: moisture content for char siew not reported in HPB Foodo as of 2026-04-16. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: char siew contains no meaningful vitamin C as the pork and sauce ingredients do not contribute ascorbic acid; confirmed consistent with HPB and USDA data. GI is an analogous estimate (GI 50, low) based on roasted pork with sweet marinade from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for char siew was found. Cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (pork, cured, roasted) as HPB did not separately report these values. Input record description referred to Char Siew Bao (steamed bun); however, the cr_id F06100012 and food_name 'Char siew' correspond to the plain barbecued pork product, not the bun. This record is scoped to char siew (barbecued pork) only. foodex2_match_type set to 'closest_parent': FoodEx2 code A0CKW (pork meat, cooked) is the closest parent category; no specific FoodEx2 code exists for char siew as a marinated roasted preparation.
